articles.title: Zeroserve: A zero-config web server you can script with eBPF
articles.summary: Zeroserve is a zero-config web server that can be scripted with eBPF, offering fast performance and efficient scripting capabilities. It allows developers to create a declarative configuration language for routing, headers, authentication, rate limiting, and proxying, while also providing an optional scripting runtime bolted on the side. This enables a single, ordinary program to see every request and decide what happens, resulting in a compact and deployable solution with no config file required.

articles.title: Ergonomic overrides for Nixpkgs
articles.summary: Ergonomic overrides for Nixpkgs are a new package that simplifies the process of overriding dependencies in Nixpkgs, which is part of a broader effort to improve usability. This project aims to address the fundamental problem of type-checking Nix, where nobody actually uses it at any significant scale, by creating embedded languages within Nix for programming "in the large". By designing better abstractions, such as override functions and overlays, developers can build more user-friendly tools that work well in inexpert hands.

articles.title: Moving beyond fork() + exec()
articles.summary: The core topic of this article is moving beyond the traditional fork() + exec() system call model, which can be expensive and inefficient due to the need to copy process state and memory. Li Chen's proposal for spawn templates aims to optimize this pattern by creating a template for an executable file that can be reused across multiple invocations, reducing the overhead of copying and discarding memory.

articles.title: Running Python code in a sandbox with MicroPython and WASM
articles.summary: Running Python code in a sandbox with MicroPython and WASM allows developers to execute arbitrary code safely within their own applications, meeting the need for plugins that extend software features without risking core application security or user data exposure. This approach enables the creation of mechanisms such as scheduled code execution, data transformation, and database updates, all while adhering to memory and CPU limits. By using MicroPython in WebAssembly, developers can build sandboxed environments that are easy to install, secure, and controlled, making it an attractive solution for various use cases.

articles.title: Benchmarks in Leipzig
articles.summary: A group of mathematicians compiled a dataset of research-level mathematics questions with known answers between April 1 and May 15, 2026, at the Max Planck Institute for Mathematics in Leipzig, Germany. The dataset consists of 100 questions that were evaluated using three stages: single attempts by five state-of-the-art large language models (LLMs), a 20-runs-per-model evaluation with three LLMs, and a 3-run attempt with two heavy-thinking models. This demonstrates the impressive mathematical reasoning capabilities of LLMs, which have become increasingly effective in solving complex problems.

articles.title: Trees to Flows and Back: Unifying Decision Trees and Diffusion Models
articles.summary: Decision trees and diffusion models are being unified by establishing a mathematical correspondence between hierarchical decision trees and diffusion processes in limiting regimes. This unification reveals a shared optimization principle known as Global Trajectory Score Matching or GTSM, which is asymptotically optimal for gradient boosting in idealized versions. The work has practical instantiations such as treeflow, a method that achieves competitive generation quality on tabular data with higher fidelity and a computational speedup, and dsmtree, a novel distillation method that transfers hierarchical decision logic into neural networks matching teacher performance within 2% on many benchmarks.

articles.title: The Smart TV in Your LivingRoom Is a Node in the AIScraping Economy
articles.summary: The work at Include Security has been collaborating with AI companies to use datacenters and training AI models, but there's another way to train these models - by scraping data from the internet using residential proxy networks. Bright Data is a company that sells access to its massive residential proxy network of 400 million home IP addresses, which can be used as exit nodes for web-scraping traffic. This means that smart TVs and other connected devices can be used as proxies to train AI models on scraped data from the internet.

articles.title: The perils of UUID primary keys in SQLite
articles.summary: Random UUIDs can cause significant extra paging in clustered indexes, leading to poor performance. This is because the random nature of UUIDs means that rows are inserted into the B-tree in a seemingly random order, requiring re-balancing which can be time-consuming. The use of random UUIDs as primary keys can lead to wasted space and increased latency in databases like SQLite.

articles.title: Total Reciprocity Public License
articles.summary: The Total Reciprocity Public License is an open-source software license that allows for free use, modification, and distribution of software without any restrictions. It was created in 2004 by Mark Shuttleworth as a response to the GNU General Public License, which restricts the ability to modify and distribute software. The TRL license has been widely adopted by developers due to its permissive terms, making it easier for companies to integrate open-source components into their products.

articles.title: An announcement from the Steering Council regarding the JIT project
articles.summary: The Steering Council is requesting a Standards Track PEP to formalize the JIT project's status in CPython, which has been undergoing significant development over several years. The JIT compiler has made real performance improvements, but its informal status on main branch means it cannot be developed further without community consensus. The council wants to discuss alternative proposals and ensure that any changes are subject to the PEP process.

articles.title: The smallest C++ binary
articles.summary: The smallest C++ binary is a program that runs successfully with no errors and produces a deterministically zero exit status. It must be created using the GCC compiler, stripped of debugging information, and produced by only linking against the standard library. The resulting binary can be as small as 13632 bytes, but it still includes some code that is not executed before entering the main function.

articles.title: Stop Using Conventional Commits
articles.summary: Conventional Commits promises to add semantic meaning to commit messages but fails to deliver on its promise by prioritizing type over scope, which is the most important part of a commit. The format typically used for Conventional Commits, where type comes first and then optional scope and description, has a major failing: it puts type ahead of scope. This means that contributors and other stakeholders who care about the scope of changes are often ignored in favor of the type of change. As a result, Conventional Commits fails to provide meaningful information for debugging, incident response, or understanding changes made in a commit.
